title: Ensure that the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments are set as appropriate

input_row.title: Ensure that the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments are set as appropriate
input_row.description: Setup TLS connection on the Kubelets.
input_row.rationale: Kubelet communication contains sensitive parameters that should remain encrypted in transit. Configure the Kubelets to serve only HTTPS traffic.
input_row.audit: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments exist and they are set as appropriate.
input_row.remediation: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and set up the TLS connection on the Kubelet. Then, edit the /etc/kubernetes/kubelet file on the master node and set the KUBELET_ARGS parameter to include "--tls-cert-file=<path/to/tls-certificate-file>" and "--tls- private-key-file=<path/to/tls-key-file>": KUBELET_ARGS="--tls-cert-file=<path/to/tls-certificate-file> --tls-private- key-file=<path/to/tls-key-file>" Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl restart kubelet.service Impact: TLS and client certificate authentication must be configured for your Kubernetes cluster deployment.   169 | P a g e Default Value: By default, --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments are not set. If -- tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file are not provided, a self-signed certificate and key are generated for the public address and saved to the directory passed to --cert-dir. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kubelet/ 2. http://rootsquash.com/2016/05/10/securing-the-kubernetes-api/ 3. https://github.com/kelseyhightower/docker-kubernetes-tls-guide
input_row.impact: TLS and client certificate authentication must be configured for your Kubernetes cluster deployment.   169 | P a g e Default Value: By default, --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments are not set. If -- tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file are not provided, a self-signed certificate and key are generated for the public address and saved to the directory passed to --cert-dir.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
This control requires verification that the kubelet process on each node is started with the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments set appropriately. These are command-line flags passed to the kubelet binary, and their presence and values are not exposed via the Kubernetes API or accessible through kubectl or any Kubernetes-native resource. The audit procedure explicitly instructs to check the running process arguments on each node (e.g., via ps -ef | grep kubelet), which requires OS-level access to each node and cannot be performed via Kubernetes API calls or kubectl.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into each node in the Kubernetes cluster.
2. Run the following command to list the kubelet process and its arguments:
   ```
   ps -ef | grep kubelet
   ```
3. Examine the output and verify that both --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments are present and set to appropriate file paths.
4. Confirm that the referenced certificate and key files exist on the node and are valid (not expired, correct permissions, etc.).
5. Repeat for all nodes in the cluster.
6. Compliance is achieved if all kubelet processes on all nodes have both arguments set to valid, appropriate files.
